item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
Short and sweet, and plenty weird!

https://youtu.be/ZJ79wZD9wGQ

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Y'know, someone could make that. SCP-682 is abandoned over there in a mine, it comes back because it can recognize the fine details of anomalies and knows exactly how to come back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He got the drones thing from Kalinin's 001 proposal, which mentioned that SCP-2627 had tons of brain harvesting drones after being effected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, 682 has been described as neither living nor dead, so how it would be affected is questionable. And where did you get that thing about the drones from? There aren't any in the article and I never conceived of any.

Glad you like it. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is weirdddd. I really like it on one hand, but on another hand you could just send SCP-682 off the bridge in a huge tussle and abandon it. On the other hand, BOARDWALK MINING TUNNELS and DRONES THAT ARE DESIGNED TO FORCIBLY HARVEST BRAIN TISSUE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one. +1

Great job of doing a lot with a little - literally, you describe a whole alternate world in a few sentences. This SCP is short and intriguing and leaves you wanting for more, but not to a fault. In that sense, it reminds me a lot of SCP-450, another one I really like, except here you actually manage to explain everything.

While we're on the subject of explaining, I also don't feel this needs anything more, as you've clearly shown what the other world is, and explained that there's no life there, which is enough info that I feel satisfied. It's refreshing to see an "alternate world" SCP that's short, and doesn't feel it has to go all Red Sea Object to be good.

I love that picture by the way, it just fits, and has this otherworldly vibe to it.

My only nitpick is the title, "Boardwalk Empire", which just feels like a tacked-on reference to the show, and isn't even accurate (there's no "Empire" anywhere in the article).

Other than that, good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this form more personally, it doesn't tell you every little detail with addendums, test logs, and etc. It leaves you to imagine your own tales.

To me, just wandering around one day to suddenly cross a bridge into an endless sea of boardwalks on an island in middle of the ocean with no exit is nightmarish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how this can be otherworldly, potentially dangerous, and yet peaceful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The image is just so fascinating. I wish that it were more story-driven, as I want to find out more about this strange dimension of interconnecting walkways, and more importantly who built them, but the fact that it leaves you wanting to learn more is a sign of its quality. It's elegant in its execution, packing a large amount of mystery in a short description.

Upvoted definitely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and notes on what the researcher writing the article had for lunch that day.

Someone write a serious article where this happens and you'll win all the Internet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[I]t connects to a similar system of boardwalks, covered bridges […]

"Oh... covered bridges..."

But seriously, nice skip with a nice, isolated "feel" (for lack of a better word).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feel free to do so! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't help but wonder if every boardwalk leads to another world.

Don't bloat the article with that sort of content after the contest, though. Just let us imagine.





Actually, I'm adding this skip to my CYOA list, if the author permits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's part of the point of the contest. Modern articles are bloated with interviews and recovered materials logs and notes on what the researcher writing the article had for lunch that day. There is beauty in simplicity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a really cool article. It's otherworldly in its implications, and it's fun to contemplate the eeriness of some random place that you happen to walk into at night and end up in crazy boardwalk realm. There's a very subtle menace that's not played up too much, but as others have stated, there's a certain tranquility that the boardwalk realm has to it that's also interesting to think about.

Another good product of the interaction of the parameters set by the contest and a clever writer. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An activation event is when SCP-2627 opens at 0017 and connects to the boardwalk world. I can reword that after the contest ends.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This does feel like it belongs among articles from years ago, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait, what's an activation event, in this context?

"Any animal tissue not on one of the walkways when SCP-2627 activates vanishes permanently."

It sounds like a discrete event - like someone first setting foot on SCP-2627's entrance. If someone enters and steps off the boardwalk in the "empire", do they disappear when someone else enters?

I want to like this, but part of me thinks, "just another spatial anomaly into a deserted world".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmh. I think I like it.

I have one major drawback though: you write in one paragraph of the description:

Between 0017 and 0023 hours, local time, traversing SCP-2627 by foot leads to a version of Ocracoke Island lacking any artificial structures, excepting an extensive network of intersecting boardwalks.

And in the next paragraph:

A single boardwalk extends from Silver Lake harbor to the mainland, where it connects to a similar system of boardwalks, covered bridges, elevated walkways, and mining tunnels. Long-distance drone exploration reveals no region of North America that is not covered by this network.

(and I should quote the stuff about 1.7 km extension into the ocean, too…)
So, shouldn't you rewrite that first quoted sentence into '…into a version of North America lacking any artificial structure', etc? It would both be more accurate, and carry considerably more oompph!

Upvoted anyway.

Plus: when I read 'Ocracoke', I first thought 'HAH! At long last an SCP about the Lost Colony!!!!'. One Google search later: the Lost Colony is Roanoke, not Ocracoke… Dammit. ;>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Generally speaking, I write more in the style of Series I than in the more story-intensive SCPs prevalent today. And I don't see anything wrong with that, so long as the SCP is evocative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only sounds are those of wind through trees and grass… The night sky is clear and deep…

Just remember to be on the path at night and you can stay as long as you want.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed the mainlist title to "Boardwalk Empire" after some thought. The show wasn't an inspiration for this, but the name fits oh so well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the fact that any animal matter not on the boardwalk when the effect activates simply disappears to be a little scary. Imagine sneaking out with a girl in the middle of the night, taking a nap, then suddenly being a missing person.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just odd enough to be interesting. I'd actually like to see this expanded later with an exploration log; I'm betting there's something really weird out there…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It feels… peaceful. I want to visit there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Adding to this, a map or graph of the general pattern would propably look awesome.

I also really like the imagery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think there's a specific name for them

Walking simulator.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was this possibly inspired by the title of a certain Steve Buscemi-starring HBO show?

Nope. I don't have HBO and have no clue what show you're talking about, unless it's maybe Boardwalk Empire. But the SCP was actually inspired by the photo, which I took on a recent vacation to Okracoke Island.

I know the kind of game-thing you're talking about, like Going Home or whatnot. The ones more artistic than play-based. I don't think there's a specific name for them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, good to know. I'll keep an eye out for those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was this possibly inspired by the title of a certain Steve Buscemi-starring HBO show?

This gives me the same kind of eerie feeling that SCP-1479 does. And just like that article, while it isn't bad, I get the feeling that this would be best appreciated in a different medium, such as maybe a video "game"-like simulation1.

Footnotes
1. What's the proper term for these? You know, programmed scenarios where you have some interactivity, but there is nothing to achieve.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do have some ideas regarding the world on the other side of the boardwalk, but I think they'd be better explored in Tales, rather than getting shoehorned into the main article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm upvoting since I really like the imagery, but I feel this could benefit from an exploration log, either by man or drone. There needn't be any spooky twist or ultra-danger shoehorned into the log, just some more interesting descriptions of Bridge-Land.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. I'm still not entirely sure what's going on here, but I like it enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is good by itself, but I wish it had something else to it, however minor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pros:

  • The imagery of broadwalk-mania is evocative and sort of fun.

Cons:

  • It describes itself well, but there's nothing else to it. It's more of early Series I works, where the article is the thing itself and most development is carried by external sources (i.e. Tales etc.)

Verdict: Neutral vote (but would like to see it being written about)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, showing fine for me. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My entry into the 2015 Short Contest.

EDIT: I uploaded the picture from my work computer, but it's still showing as a broken image to me. Let me know if that's happening for anyone else & I'll fix it when I get home after work.

EDIT: Btw, I took the picture myself and hereby release it under the standard CC license for SCP stuffs.
